title: Castle in the Air - Chapter Seven: Which introduces the genie (Fragment 2)
text:
Somewhat to his surprise, Abdullah found he was far more angry than frightened. Perhaps it was that he had exhausted all his fear that morning in front of the Sultan. Or perhaps it was just because he ached all over. He was sore and scraped from sliding down the sand dune, and one of his ankle bands was chafing brutally. “But I have told you,” he said haughtily, “that I shall be no use to you until my chains are off.”

“It is not magic we want from you. It is knowledge,” said Kabul Aqba. He beckoned to the man who had gone wading into the pool. “Tell us what manner of thing this is,” he said, “and we may let your legs loose as a reward.”

The man who had been in the pool squatted down and held out a smoky blue bottle with a rounded belly. Abdullah levered himself to his elbows and looked at it resentfully. It seemed to be new. There was a clean new cork showing through the smoky glass of the neck, which had been sealed over with a stamped lead seal, again new-looking. It looked like a bottle of perfume that had lost its label. “It’s quite light,” said the squatting man, shaking the bottle about, “and it neither rattles nor sloshes.”

Abdullah thought of a way he could use this to get himself unchained. “It’s a genie bottle,” he said. “Know, denizens of the desert, that it could be very dangerous. Do but take these chains from me, and I will control the genie within and make sure he obeys your every wish. Otherwise I think no man should touch it.”

The man holding the bottle dropped it nervously, but Kabul Aqba only laughed and picked it up. “It looks more like something good to drink,” he said. He tossed the flask to another man. “Open it.” The man laid down his saber and got out a large knife, with which he hacked at the lead seal.

Abdullah saw his chance of getting unchained going. Worse, he was about to be exposed as a fraud. “It is really extremely dangerous, O rubies among robbers,” he protested. “Once you have broken the seal, do not on any account draw the cork.” As he spoke, the man peeled the seal away and dropped it on the sand. He began prying the cork out, while another man held the bottle steady for him. “If you must draw the cork,” Abdullah babbled, “at least tap on the bottle the correct and mystical number of times and make the genie inside swear—”

The cork came out. Pop . A thin mauvish vapor came smoking out of the neck of the flask. Abdullah hoped the thing was full of poison. But the vapor almost instantly thickened to a cloud that came rushing out of the bottle like a kettle boiling bluish mauve steam. This steam shaped itself into a face—large and angry and blue—and arms, and a wisp of body connected to the bottle, and went on rushing forth until it was easily ten feet tall.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Seven: Which introduces the genie

vocabulary.definition: Irritated or worn by rubbing.
vocabulary.example: One of his ankle bands was chafing brutally.

vocabulary.definition: Metal links used to restrain or bind someone.
vocabulary.example: That I shall be no use to you until my chains are off.

vocabulary.definition: A stopper made usually from the bark of the cork oak tree, used to close bottles.
vocabulary.example: Once you have broken the seal, do not on any account draw the cork.

vocabulary.definition: Inhabitants or occupants of a particular place.
vocabulary.example: Know, denizens of the desert, that it could be very dangerous.

vocabulary.definition: Completely used up or very tired; drained of energy or resources.
vocabulary.example: Perhaps it was that he had exhausted all his fear that morning in front of the Sultan.

vocabulary.definition: A person who deceives others by pretending to be something they are not.
vocabulary.example: Worse, he was about to be exposed as a fraud.

vocabulary.definition: A supernatural being believed to grant wishes, often enclosed in a bottle.
vocabulary.example: “It’s a genie bottle,” he said.

vocabulary.definition: Cut or chopped roughly or violently.
vocabulary.example: He hacked at the lead seal.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing disdain or arrogance; proudly or arrogantly.
vocabulary.example: “But I have told you,” he said haughtily, “that I shall be no use to you until my chains are off.”

vocabulary.definition: Information, understanding, or skills gained through experience or education.
vocabulary.example: It is not magic we want from you. It is knowledge.

vocabulary.definition: Raised or moved oneself using leverage, often with arms.
vocabulary.example: Abdullah levered himself to his elbows and looked at it resentfully.

vocabulary.definition: The use of supernatural powers to influence events.
vocabulary.example: It is not magic we want from you. It is knowledge.

vocabulary.definition: A way or type of thing; kind or type.
vocabulary.example: Tell us what manner of thing this is.

vocabulary.definition: Relating to mysticism or supernatural phenomena.
vocabulary.example: At least tap on the bottle the correct and mystical number of times.

vocabulary.definition: To follow commands or directions.
vocabulary.example: I will control the genie within and make sure he obeys your every wish.

vocabulary.definition: Forced open or removed something with effort, often using leverage.
vocabulary.example: He began prying the cork out, while another man held the bottle steady.

vocabulary.definition: With bitterness or indignation at having been treated unfairly.
vocabulary.example: Abdullah levered himself to his elbows and looked at it resentfully.

vocabulary.definition: A metaphorical phrase implying something precious among thieves; used figuratively.
vocabulary.example: “It is really extremely dangerous, O rubies among robbers,” he protested.

vocabulary.definition: Moving forward rapidly and with force.
vocabulary.example: This steam shaped itself into a face and went on rushing forth.

vocabulary.definition: A type of curved, single-edged sword.
vocabulary.example: The man laid down his saber and got out a large knife.

vocabulary.definition: Having a surface that is injured or abraded by scraping.
vocabulary.example: He was sore and scraped from sliding down the sand dune.

vocabulary.definition: Closed securely so that nothing can enter or escape.
vocabulary.example: There was a clean new cork showing through the smoky glass of the neck, which had been sealed over with a stamped lead seal.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling pain or discomfort, often from injury.
vocabulary.example: He was sore and scraped from sliding down the sand dune.

vocabulary.definition: Became denser or more concentrated.
vocabulary.example: But the vapor almost instantly thickened to a cloud.

vocabulary.definition: A substance diffused or suspended in the air, especially a gas or mist.
vocabulary.example: A thin mauvish vapor came smoking out of the neck of the flask.

vocabulary.definition: A small thin or twisted bunch, piece, or amount of something.
vocabulary.example: And a wisp of body connected to the bottle.
